text: Five trocars, including four robotic trocars, are inserted into the abdominal cavity. The surgeon performs an episcopic evaluation, revealing significant visceral obesity and a narrow pelvis. The transdental approach is initiated for the total mesorectal excision (TME) due to anticipated technical difficulties.

The surgeon initiates interstitial resection at the dentate line using a Lone Star retractor. An intrastringent resection of the internal anal sphincter is performed over a two-centimeter distance. The TEO endoscopic platform is transiently inserted, and the rectum is insufflated with CO₂. Additional intrastringent resection is carried out endoscopically using a bipolar device.

The surgeon performs a third viperspring dissection, revealing the anatomical plane between the rectum and prostate anteriorly, and reaching the presacral space posteriorly. The rectum is then occluded endoscopically using a viperspring suture with the endostitch device.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the rectum and mesorectum, sharply dissecting the plane between the anterior rectal wall and the posterior prostate using cautery and a bipolar device. The dissection is extended laterally, ensuring the preservation of the mesorectum and protection of the lateral pelvic-side wall nerves.

The surgeon completes circumferential mobilization of the rectum and mesorectum, sharply dissecting cephalad towards the peritoneal cavity. Anteriorly, the prostate and seminal vesicles are preserved, while posteriorly, dissection proceeds along the presacral plane.

The surgeon reaches and divides the peritoneal reflection, confirming entry into the peritoneal cavity from below. Circumferential dissection of the mesorectum and rectum is completed transiently, but progress is limited due to poor exposure and lack of traction from above.

The four robotic arms are docked into position for the robotic portion of the total mesorectal excision (TME). The sigmoid colon is elevated to place the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) under tension, which is then scored with cautery and carefully dissected using the robotic arms.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) after full mobilization. The sigmoid mesentery is dissected with the robotic sealer, completing the medial to lateral mobilization.

The surgeon initiates pelvic dissection using a transdental endoscopic approach to mobilize residual rectal and mesorectal attachments. The previous plane of dissection is reached, and the residual attachments are further mobilized from a robotic abdominal approach, completely freeing the rectum.

The robotic arms are removed, and the surgeon performs splenic flexure mobilization using a laparoscopic approach. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is dissected, and the lateral attachments of the proximal colon are completely divided. The specimen is exteriorized transiently with laparoscopic assistance, ensuring no twists occur in the colon or its mesentery during extraction.
The surgeon transects the specimen transanally and performs a hand-sewn colloidal anastomosis. A laparoscopic loop ileostomy is created, and a pelvic drain is visibly left in place.
